comment.body:
Saw this interesting story : In an underground warehouse somewhere in Huaqiangbei, Shenzhen, China a transaction is taking place:

A Chinese toy manufacturer is “selling” smart toys with an actual value of 10 million USD to an American chip company at a price of 1 million USD.
In return, an American chip manufacturer is providing AI chips worth 10 million USD to the same Chinese toy company, also at a price of 1 million USD.

Both parties quietly record only the discounted amounts in their cross-border accounts. The goods are then resold domestically through intermediaries at regular market prices as “imported goods.”

This kind of transaction used to be the stuff of urban legends told in hushed tones, but now it’s becoming commonplace—playing out again and again under the looming threat of Trump’s tariffs. It’s expanding from just U.S.-China trade to a much broader global scale, reshaping international commerce.

“Business is still being done, just more complicated now,” said Zhong Wete, head of exports for a toy company in Dongguan. He revealed that his company had reached a “secret agreement” with a second-tier U.S. supplier to carry out this kind of offshore value-matching through a third-party offshore company.

In just the first half of 2026 alone, this approach helped them evade more than 30 million USD in taxes.

This kind of transaction used to be the stuff of urban legends told in hushed tones, but now it’s becoming commonplace—playing out again and again under the looming threat of Trump’s tariffs. It’s expanding from just U.S.-China trade to a much broader global scale, reshaping international commerce.

The more pain can be tolerated aspect for the US looks like it’s not all it was made out to be at first glance.

comment.body: If Trump folds too quickly, the article suggests it’s a clear win for China in the immediate “poker game.” Xi’s strategy of dragging out the conflict until U.S. confidence cracks would succeed, leaving the U.S. economically and geopolitically weaker. However, the broader fallout—global instability, awakened conflicts, or a U.S. backlash—might complicate China’s victory over time. For now, though, Xi would likely see it as a hand well played.

comment.body:
I not sure how the current situation works out well for China. They have real estate crash continuing. A huge misallocation of capital creating 90 million empty homes which with a falling population will never be occupied. Enough empty homes to house 3/4 of US population. Massive.  Housing and associated production represents 30% of Chinas economy. House building has dropped dramatically 

To compensate for the huge destruction of wealth in building useless buildings now they are building useless cars, China currently has the capacity to make 49 million cars/light vehicles. That is roughly 50% of global demand. They have the capacity to make 800 million tonnes of steel…. 10x the USA which is an economy 30-40% larger.

China does not have markets for all this stuff just like they did not have markets for the housing. They just continue to misallocate capital. It’s a giant deck of cards
